source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Recognise that living things can be grouped in a variety of ways; explore and use classification keys to help group, identify and name a variety of living things in their local and wider environment; recognise that environments can change and that this can sometimes pose dangers to living things.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack uses observation cards, branching classification keys and habitat-change evidence to group living things respectfully and explain environmental effects without encouraging unsafe field handling.

objective.child_goal: I can use careful observations to classify living things and explain how habitat changes can affect them.
objective.statement: Group living things using observable features, use classification keys to identify organisms, and explain how environmental changes can sometimes pose dangers.
misconceptions: Groups living things by where they are found or personal preference instead of observable features.
Treats classification keys as guessing games rather than yes/no evidence decisions.
Assumes all insects are pests or all small animals are insects.
Thinks habitat change is always immediately fatal or always harmless.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Putting living things into groups using shared features.
vocabulary.audio_script: Classification means grouping living things by features they share.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Organism cards sorted into feature-labelled trays.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A set of yes or no questions that helps identify a living thing.
vocabulary.audio_script: A classification key uses questions to guide you to a name or group.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A branching path with yes and no arrows.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Something you can observe about a living thing, such as wings, petals or a backbone.
vocabulary.audio_script: A feature is an observable clue you can use for grouping.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Close-up callouts showing legs, leaves, shell and wings.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The place where a living thing lives and gets what it needs.
vocabulary.audio_script: A habitat is the place where a living thing lives and finds what it needs.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A pond, woodland and school garden with food, shelter and water icons.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A change to surroundings, such as less water, less shelter, pollution or new building work.
vocabulary.audio_script: Environmental change means surroundings have changed in a way that can affect living things.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Before and after habitat cards with one changed condition highlighted.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Look closely. Which features can you observe and use for grouping?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Focus learners on observable evidence before naming or classifying.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Scientists group living things using features they can observe carefully.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Local organism cards show zoomable features with no group labels at first.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Living things can be grouped in more than one useful way.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce classification as evidence-based grouping rather than one fixed classroom sort.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: One sort might use backbone or no backbone. Another might use flowering or non-flowering plants. The feature must match the group.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The same cards are sorted by different valid feature rules.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Answer each yes or no question using the evidence on the card.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model classification-key navigation as a sequence of observable decisions.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: At each branch, answer only the question you can check. Follow the yes or no path.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A ladybird card moves through a simple key: animal, has legs, six legs, hard wing cases.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Build a simple key that separates four living things using clear yes or no questions.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Move from using keys to designing keys with unambiguous observable features.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Choose a question that splits the cards. Then choose the next question for the smaller group.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A drag-and-drop branching key builder checks whether every endpoint identifies one card.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: The pond becomes shaded and has less plant growth. Which living things might be affected, and what evidence supports your idea?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect specific environmental changes to likely needs of organisms without overclaiming.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A habitat change can affect food, shelter, water or light. Use the evidence before deciding.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Before and after pond evidence cards show light, plants, insects and shelter indicators.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach your companion why 'I do not like it' is not a scientific classification feature.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair subjective grouping and promote respectful language about living things.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A useful classification feature is something others can observe and check.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A companion tries to group mini-beasts by scary/not scary, then revises to number of legs.

manipulatives.purpose: Group local and wider-environment living things by observable features, with more than one valid grouping route.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners inspect cards, reveal zoomed features, choose a grouping rule and justify why each organism belongs.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Feature callouts use labels, icons and texture patterns; no essential information is colour-only; organism images have alt text and simplified line-art mode.

manipulatives.purpose: Use and build yes/no classification keys that identify living things from observable evidence.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners follow a key step by step, then assemble questions to separate a small organism set.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Keyboard navigation follows branch order; each yes/no decision is announced; reduced-motion mode uses static branch highlighting and a text-list equivalent.

manipulatives.purpose: Compare before/after habitat evidence and reason cautiously about possible effects on living things.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners select changed conditions, match them to organism needs and choose evidence-supported impact statements.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Before/after panels can be viewed one at a time; impact icons have text labels; no distressing images of harmed animals are used.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave feature sorting and key use before habitat-change reasoning; use retrieval to revisit respectful, observable classification features.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Group living things by shared observable features rather than preference, danger or location alone.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: organism cards + visible feature set + choose grouping rule + justify placements
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use respectful language for all organisms and avoid culturally loaded labels such as good/bad or useful/useless.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Use yes/no classification keys accurately by answering only from observable evidence.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: organism image/card + branching yes/no key + endpoint identification + path check
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keys must be unambiguous from the shown evidence and avoid requiring specialist taxonomic knowledge.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Construct simple keys that separate a small set of organisms using clear observable questions.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: four organism cards + question bank + branch builder + endpoint validation + revise prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Accept multiple valid keys when every branch question is observable and each endpoint is unique.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain possible effects of environmental changes using organism needs and before/after evidence.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: habitat before/after + changed condition + organism needs + supported impact statement
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use cautious language such as may, might and could; do not present distressing outcomes or blame children.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain vocabulary, feature evidence, key navigation and habitat-change reasoning through mixed review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: short retrieval queue weighted by prior errors across sort, key and habitat formats
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep repeated-review items brief, static by default and fully text-supported.

body.prompt: Which grouping rule is scientific and checkable for these living things?
choices: Has a shell or does not have a shell
Looks friendly or does not look friendly
Is nice or is not nice
I have seen it before or I have not seen it before

expected_answer.value: Has a shell or does not have a shell

hints: A scientific grouping rule should use an observable feature.
Different people should be able to check the same feature.
question_variants.explanation: Having a shell is an observable feature. Friendly, nice and familiar are opinions or experiences, not reliable classification features.

body.prompt: Use the key to identify the organism: animal; has legs; has six legs; has hard wing cases.
key_path: Does it have hard wing cases?
choices: ladybird
earthworm
snail
fern

hints: Follow the yes path only when the feature is shown.
Six legs and hard wing cases are clues.
question_variants.explanation: The card follows the animal, legs, six legs and hard wing cases path, which identifies it as a ladybird in this key.

body.prompt: You need a yes/no question to separate a snail from a spider, beetle and bee. Which question is clearest?
choices: Does it have a shell?
Is it interesting?
Is it quite small?
Does it live somewhere nice?

hints: A useful key question has a clear yes or no answer.
Choose a feature you can see on the card.
question_variants.explanation: A shell is an observable feature that separates the snail from the other shown organisms. The other questions are unclear or opinion-based.

body.prompt: A pond has much less plant growth after nearby trees make it very shaded. Which evidence-supported effect is most likely?
organism_needs: pond snails use plants for food and shelter
some insects lay eggs near pond plants
choices: Some pond animals may have less food or shelter
All pond animals will definitely die today
No living thing can be affected by less plant growth
The animals become plants

expected_answer.value: Some pond animals may have less food or shelter

hints: Use the organism needs shown in the evidence.
Cautious words such as may or might are useful when evidence is limited.
question_variants.explanation: Less plant growth could reduce food or shelter for some pond animals. The evidence supports a possible effect, not a certain immediate outcome for every animal.

body.prompt: A learner says, 'Bees are pests, so they should be in a bad animals group.' Which scientific response is best?
choices: Group it using observable features such as six legs and wings
Use bad animals because everyone agrees
Do not classify bees because they move
Group it by whether one person is scared of it

expected_answer.value: Group it using observable features such as six legs and wings

hints: Classification should be respectful and based on evidence.
Look for features that others can check.
question_variants.explanation: Scientific classification uses observable features, not labels such as bad or scary. Six legs and wings are checkable evidence.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Groups living things by where they are found or personal preference instead of observable features.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses groups such as scary, nice, garden only or favourite without a shared feature.
repair_sequence: Ask whether another scientist could check the grouping rule from the card.
Highlight observable features such as legs, shell, wings, leaves, flowers or backbone.
Resort the same cards using one chosen observable feature.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner states a checkable feature and uses it consistently on a new set of cards.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats classification keys as guessing games rather than yes/no evidence decisions.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner jumps to a familiar organism name without following the key path.
repair_sequence: Cover the endpoint names and answer only the first yes/no question.
Reveal one branch at a time and require evidence for each answer.
Replay the completed path and compare it with the organism features.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner follows all branches in order and can justify each yes/no choice from the card.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Assumes all insects are pests or all small animals are insects.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner labels spiders, worms or snails as insects, or uses pest language as a classification group.
repair_sequence: Compare leg number, body clues and other visible features across small animals.
Replace pest/not pest with observable questions such as has six legs.
Use respectful language prompts and a new mini-beast key.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner distinguishes insects from other small animals using observable features and avoids value labels.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks habitat change is always immediately fatal or always harmless.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects extreme outcomes without matching changed conditions to organism needs.
repair_sequence: Identify the changed condition in the habitat.
Match the change to food, water, shelter, light or breeding needs.
Choose a cautious impact statement using may, might or could.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains a possible effect using specific evidence from organism needs and habitat change.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm habitat scenes, no sudden animal movement and no distressing injury, death or predator imagery.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Use static organism cards, step-by-step key highlighting and before/after habitat panels instead of animated movement.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Narrate organism features and key questions with replay; do not require identifying species from audio.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Pair vocabulary with icons, use yes/no buttons, provide sentence frames and keep organism names decodable where possible.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Show only the current key branch or habitat evidence panel; hide unused cards until needed.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow zoom, pause and backtracking in keys; preserve previous answers so learners can revise without restarting.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat reclassification as improving the key, not being wrong; validate careful observation before correction.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach classify, feature, key, yes, no, habitat, environment, change, food, shelter, water and light with paired images.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires grouping by observable features, following a simple classification key and identifying a habitat change from evidence. Secure requires building or revising a clear key and explaining possible habitat impacts using organism needs with cautious scientific language across retained sessions.
evidence.parent_summary: Can classify living things using observable features, use yes/no keys and explain how habitat changes may affect living things.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Separates feature selection, key-path decisions and habitat-impact explanations so teachers can see whether classifications are evidence-based and respectful.
next_actions: Route subjective grouping errors to observable-feature resorting.
Route key guessing to one-branch-at-a-time evidence checks.
Route extreme habitat claims to organism-needs matching and cautious language.
Use local-environment retrieval with images that do not require handling real organisms.

qa.notes: Review-stage proof content. Human review should verify organism feature sets, local/wider-environment examples, respectful language, habitat-impact claims and SEND alternatives before promotion.
